elements.sent-2411-001: Given an initial video from a text-to-video generation model, \ours{ refines video in four stages: (1) video evaluation, (2) refinement planning, (3) region decomposition, and (4) localized refinement.}
elements.sent-2411-002: The black-white mask in the bottom left of each example indicates the localized refinement plan (black: regions to preserve / white: regions to refine).. \end{center ].}
elements.sent-2411-003: Recent text-to-video (T2V) diffusion models have demonstrated impressive generation capabilities across various domains.
elements.sent-2411-004: However, these models often generate videos that have misalignments with text prompts, especially when the prompts describe complex scenes with multiple objects and attributes.
elements.sent-2411-005: To address this, we introduce \ours{ , a novel model-agnostic, training-free video refinement framework that automatically identifies fine-grained text-video misalignments and generates explicit spatial and textual feedback, enabling a T2V diffusion model to perform targeted, localized refinements.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In (2) refinement planning, we identify accurately generated objects and then create localized prompts to refine other areas in the video.
elements.sent-2411-007: Next, in (3) region decomposition, we segment the correctly generated area using a combined grounding module.
elements.sent-2411-008: We regenerate the video by adjusting the misaligned regions while preserving the correct regions in (4) localized refinement.
elements.sent-2411-009: On two popular video generation benchmarks (EvalCrafter and T2V-CompBench), \ours{ substantially outperforms recent baselines across various text-video alignment metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-010: We provide a comprehensive analysis of \ours{ components and qualitative examples.}

elements.sent-2411-032: These methods train a denoising model that can gradually generate clear videos from noisy videos, where the noises are added via diffusion process~\cite{ho2020denoising .}
elements.sent-2411-033: The denoising is commonly performed in a compact latent space of an autoencoder~\cite{rombach2022high for computational efficiency.}
elements.sent-2411-034: VideoCrafter2~\cite{chen2024videocrafter2 synthesizes low-quality videos with high-quality images through a joint training design of spatial and temporal modules, obtaining high-quality videos.}
elements.sent-2411-035: T2V-turbo~\cite{li2024t2v presents a distilled video consistency model~\cite{wang2023videolcmvideolatentconsistency,song2023consistency for improved and rapid video generation.}}

elements.sent-2411-037: However, even the recent T2V diffusion models suffer from misalignment problems.
elements.sent-2411-038: In the following, we discuss the research direction of refining the image/video diffusion models, including \ours{ .}
elements.paragraph-2411-002: Automatic refinement for image/video diffusion models.
elements.sent-2411-039: Recent works propose refinement frameworks that automatically improve diffusion models' text alignment~\cite{sun2023dreamsync,li2024selma, opt2i,sld .}
elements.sent-2411-040: A line of work studies training-based refinement, where they detect errors of a diffusion model, generate training data, and then finetune the model to improve alignment~\cite{sun2023dreamsync,li2024selma .}
elements.sent-2411-041: However, training-based methods are expensive and can often make the model overfit to specific domains of generated training data.
elements.sent-2411-042: Another line of work proposes training-free refinement~\cite{opt2i,sld .}

elements.figure-2411-003: {\includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{fig/method_final_v3.pdf}} \caption{ Illustration of \textbf{\ours{}}. \ours{} refines the generated video in four stages: (1) video evaluation (\cref{sec:subsec:method_video_critique}), (2) refinement planning (\cref{sec:subsec:retention}), (3) region decomposition (\cref{sec:subsec:method_error_localization}), and (4) localized refinement (\cref{sec:subsec:method_refinement}). From the initial prompt $p$, we first generate a fine-grained evaluation question set $Q^o$ and ask MLLM to answer it. Next, we identify $O^*$ by MLLM and plan how to refine the other region by LLM. Using $O^*$, we decompose the region by Molmo and Semantic SAM to get the region to keep. In the end, we conduct localized refinement by the original frozen T2V model. } \label{fig:main1}
elements.sent-2411-047: We propose \ours{ , a novel automatic refinement framework for T2V generation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Below, we describe the problem definition and details of each stage.
elements.sent-2411-049: Our primary goal is to generate a video that achieves improved alignment with text prompts, using a pre-trained T2V diffusion model without requiring additional fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-050: Given an input text prompt $p$ and initial noise $\epsilon_0\sim\mathcal{N}\left(0,\boldsymbol{I}\right)$, we first generate an initial video $V_0=f(p, \epsilon_0)$.
elements.sent-2411-051: To identify which elements of the prompt are misaligned with the generated video, we create a list of evaluation questions designed to be answered with ``yes'' or ``No.'' Specifically, we generate object-centric questions by providing manually written in-context examples to the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-052: Following DSG~\citep{dsg , we first define semantic categories consisting of entities, attributes, and relationships.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Each element is represented as a semantic tuple $T$: attributes are expressed in 2-tuples (entity, its attribute,~(\eg{ , $\{bed, blue\}$), and relationships are in 3-tuples (subject entity, object entity, and their relationship, (\eg, $\{people, pizza, make\}$).}
elements.sent-2411-054: Based on $T$, which covers all scene-relevant information, we generate questions $Q$ using the LLM (\eg{ , GPT-4).}
elements.sent-2411-055: Note that although DSG includes ``count'' questions, they are only generated when there is more than one object class; \ie{ , there is no penalty about object counts when there is a single object in the prompt.}
elements.sent-2411-056: For example, given a prompt `there is a bear', DSG only generates an evaluation question ``is there a bear?'', which only checks the bear's existence, but does not penalize when more than one bear is generated.
elements.sent-2411-057: To address this, we edit in-context examples of DSG so that it can generate count-related questions for a single object (\eg{ , `Is there one bear?') as well, as visualized in \cref{fig:main1 step 1.}}
elements.sent-2411-058: We denote our modified DSG as \dsgours{ , $Q_c$ as count-related questions, and attribute/relationship-related questions as $Q_a$.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Our ablation study (see \Cref{tab:ablation_components ) shows that evaluation questions of \dsgours{ are more effective than the original DSG questions in guiding video refinements.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: We now evaluate the generated video $V_0$ to identify elements that are accurately generated and those that require refinement.
elements.sent-2411-062: We first define the set of key object names in $p$ from entity tuples and group questions in $Q$ corresponding to each object.
elements.sent-2411-063: Let $Q^o=\{Q^o_a, Q^o_c\}\subset Q$ be the set of questions related to object $O$ (\eg, bear), including both attribute-related ($Q^o_a$) and count-related questions ($Q^o_c$), each requiring a distinct answer format.
elements.sent-2411-064: For example, `Are the people making pizza?' corresponds to $Q^o_a$ (attribute-related question), and `Is there one bear?' corresponds to $Q_c^o$ (count-related question).
elements.sent-2411-065: Specifically, for attribute-related questions $Q^o_a$, we prompt GPT-4o to analyze the initial video $V_0$ and provide a binary answer (yes=1 and no=0), denoted as $A_a^o=\{b_a^o\}$, to evaluate the alignment between the objects in $V_0$ and those specified in the prompt $p$.
elements.sent-2411-066: For count-related questions $Q_c^o$, we generate the exact number of objects and a binary answer in the form of a triplet $A_c^o=\{b_c^o, n^o_p, n^o_v\}$, where $n_p^o$ and $n_v^o$ are the numbers of the object $O$ in $p$ and $V_0$.
elements.sent-2411-068: For example, in \cref{fig:main1 , for the question ``Is there one bear?'' $b^{\text{bear _c=0$ when $n^{\text{bear _p=1$ and $n^{\text{bear _v=2$ (when the video $V_0$ shows two bears and the prompt describes only one bear, it is marked incorrect).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: In this way, we can quantitatively assess both the presence and count of essential objects, enabling us to detect any discrepancies between the objects specified in $V_0$ and $p$.
elements.sent-2411-070: Here, we use the first frame of $V_0$ as input for GPT-4o.
elements.subsection-2411-002: Refinement Planning: what to keep and refine
elements.paragraph-2411-005: Identifying visual content to be retained.
elements.sent-2411-071: As mentioned earlier, the initially generated video may suffer from insufficient text-video alignment due to incorrect or distorted generation of objects and their attributes.
elements.sent-2411-072: However, this does not mean that all components are mis-generated; some key concepts or objects may have been generated precisely in certain areas.
elements.sent-2411-073: To this end, we first identify the key object $O^*$ and determine the number of its instances to be preserved.
elements.sent-2411-074: First, to select which object (\ie, object name) should be retained, we prompt GPT-4o with question-answer pairs and the initial video as input, allowing it to identify the most suitable object to preserve.
elements.sent-2411-075: Next, to determine the number of instances of $O^*$ to retain, we define the count of $O^*$ as $N^*$ based on the previous triplet $A^{o^* _c=\{b^{o^* _c, n^{o^* _p, n^{o^* _v\}$ as follows: \begin{equation}N^*=\begin{cases}n_p^{o^*} \text{if}\; n^{o^*}_p\leq n^{o^*}_v n^{o^*}_v \text{otherwise,} \end{cases}\end{equation} where $n_p^{o^* <n_v^{o^*}$ indicates the need to remove excess instances of the object, and $n^{o^* _p>n^{o^* _v$ suggests that additional instances are required.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: For example, in step 2 of \cref{fig:main1 , if $O^*$ represents a bear with $n^{o^* _p=1$ and $n^{o^* _v=2$, we set $N^*=1$, preserving only one bear because there is an extra bear in the generated content ($n^{o^* _p<n^{o^* _v$), requiring the deletion of an extra instance.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: This approach effectively removes the additional instance, ensuring accurate object representation.
elements.paragraph-2411-006: Prompt regeneration for regions requiring refinement.
elements.sent-2411-078: We additionally generate a local prompt for refinement to enable distinct control over different regions during generation.
elements.sent-2411-079: To this end, we prompt an LLM to produce a refinement-oriented prompt, $p^r$, based on $Q$ but excluding any questions related to $O^*$.
elements.sent-2411-080: As illustrated in \cref{fig:main1 , steps 2 and 4, this regenerated local prompt will be used to guide the denoising process for specific areas to be refined during video generation in a later stage (will be discussed in \cref{sec:subsec:method_refinement ).}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Given the errors identified in earlier stages, we localize the video regions corresponding to the errors to create concrete guidance in the following refinement stage.
elements.sent-2411-082: Similarly, SLD~\cite{sld uses an open-vocabulary object detector to detect localization errors through bounding boxes.}
elements.sent-2411-083: However, unlike T2I generation, T2V generation often introduces complex distortions (\eg{ , attribute mixing) across scenes, making it difficult to capture all cases of distortion using only a model-based detector.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Additionally, segmenting each object area with separate bounding boxes complicates interactions between objects in the scene.
elements.sent-2411-085: To address these challenges, we propose an alternative approach: identifying and retaining correctly generated objects while regenerating misaligned objects in the remaining areas.
elements.sent-2411-086: To localize the correctly generated $O^*$ area, we generate a local mask for the area to be refined using Molmo~\cite{deitke2024molmo , a VLM that can localize specific objects by referring 2d points in an image given a text-pointing prompt, and Semantic-SAM~\cite{li2023semantic .}}
elements.sent-2411-087: As illustrated in step 3 of \cref{fig:main1 , we first construct a pointing prompt $p^p$ using predefined $N^*$ and $O^*$, ``Point $\{N^*\}$ $\{O^*\}$'' (\eg, ``Point 1 bear'') and obtain the referring 2D coordinates for $O^*$ with Molmo.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Then, using this point as input, we employ Semantic-SAM to segment the specified area and finally represent it as a binary segmentation mask $\mathbf{M}\in\mathbb{R ^{H \times W}$, where $H$ and $W$ denotes the height and width of the video frame, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-089: At this stage, we refine the video to achieve a more accurately aligned output video.
elements.sent-2411-090: Inspired by region-based text-to-image generation framework~\cite{bar2023multidiffusion,li2023gligen , we adopt a mask-based segmentation approach to control specific regions within the video generation process.}
elements.sent-2411-091: Given the region to refine $\mathbf{M}$ (obtained in~\cref{sec:subsec:method_error_localization ), we introduce a selective noise re-sampling process to enable controlled regeneration of specific regions.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Specifically, different from MultiDiffusion~\cite{bar2023multidiffusion , we preserve the partial region of the initial noise map $\epsilon_0$ detected by $\mathbf{M}$ and re-initialize the rest areas with a newly sampled noise $\epsilon_0' \sim \mathcal{N (0, \mathbf{I )$ to expect different generation tendency from initial video.}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: This aims to keep the masked areas in the video consistent while allowing the unmasked areas to be refined based on the updated prompt.
elements.sent-2411-094: To process the pixel-level mask $\mathbf{M}$ in the latent space, we transform $\mathbf{M}$ from pixel space to latent space through block averaging (\ie{ , pooling).}
elements.sent-2411-095: Specifically, we take the mean of each $|H/c| \times |W/c|$ submatrix within $\mathbf{M}$, where $c$ denotes the downsampling scale factor, effectively reducing the spatial resolution of the mask.
elements.sent-2411-096: We apply this transformed mask consistently across the entire temporal domain.
elements.sent-2411-097: The combined noise map $\epsilon_0^*$ is then computed as follows: \begin{equation}\epsilon^*_0 = \left(\epsilon_0 \otimes \text{pool}\left(\mathbf{M}, c\right)\right) + \left(\epsilon'_0 \otimes \left(1 - \text{pool}\left(\mathbf{M}, c\right)\right)\right),\end{equation} where $\text{pool (\cdot, c): \mathbb{R ^{H\times W}\rightarrow\mathbb{R ^{|H/c| \times |W/c|}$ denotes the block averaging (\ie{ , pooling) operation with scale factor $c$, and $\otimes$ represents element-wise multiplication, which preserves the initial noise map structure in the masked regions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Using this hybrid noise map $\epsilon_0^*$ as input to a frozen video diffusion model with corresponding localized prompts, we achieve targeted refinement within designated regions, resulting in high-fidelity video with controlled updates that align with the intended modifications.. {.}
elements.sent-2411-099: We apply distinct text prompts to regions based on their noise re-initialization status, using $1-\mathbf{M}$ for re-initialized areas and $\mathbf{M}$ for preserved regions.
elements.sent-2411-100: For the re-initialized regions, we guide generation in the latent space using regenerated prompts $p^r$ (See~\cref{sec:subsec:retention ) tailored to those areas.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Meanwhile, motivated by the presence of noise bias~\cite{noise2, noise3, noise4 (or referred to as trigger patches~\cite{noise1 ) tied to specific text prompts in diffusion models, we reuse the initial prompt $p$ to ensure that features associated with $O^*$ are preserved in the regions designated for retention (\cref{fig:main1 , step 4).}}}

elements.sent-2411-256: Given an initially generated video that may exhibit misalignment with the input prompt, \ours{ proposes a novel automatic refinement framework for T2V generation by addressing two key questions: (i) which prompt elements are misaligned, and which objects should be preserved? (ii) which video regions should be modified, and how can we update the video? In \ours{ , we explicitly detect, localize, and address the fine-grained errors of T2V generation models in a two-stage process: (1) video refinement planning (\cref{sec:subsec:video_refinement_planning ), (2) localized refinement (\cref{sec:subsec:localized_refinement ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-257: Below, we describe the details of each stage.
elements.sent-2411-258: Our primary goal is to generate a video that achieves improved alignment with text prompts, using a pre-trained T2V diffusion model $f(\cdot)$ without requiring additional fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-259: Given an input text prompt $p$ and initial noise $\epsilon_0\sim\mathcal{N}\left(0,\boldsymbol{I}\right)$, we first generate an initial video $V_0 = f(p, \epsilon_0)$, where $V_0 \in \mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times T}$.}
elements.sent-2411-260: Here, $H$, $W$, and $T$ represent the height, width, and number of frames of the generated video, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-261: Each element is represented as a semantic tuple $\mathcal{T}$, attributes are expressed in 2-tuples (entity, its attribute,~(\eg{ , $\{bed, blue\}$), and relationships are in 3-tuples (subject entity, object entity, and their relationship, (\eg, $\{people, pizza, make\}$).}
elements.sent-2411-262: Based on $\mathcal{T}$, which covers all scene-relevant information, we generate questions $Q$ using the LLM.
elements.sent-2411-263: Next, we prompt the MLLM (\eg, GPT-4o) to generate a binary answer $b$ for evaluation of the initial video $V_0$.
elements.sent-2411-264: To represent $V_0$ as input for the MLLM, we uniformly sample four frames and concatenate them into a single image.
elements.sent-2411-265: Given the $V_0$ in this concatenated form and count-related questions $Q_c^o$, the model outputs precise object count and a binary answer in the form of a triplet $A_c^o=\{b_c^o, n^o_p, n^o_v\}$, where $n_p^o$ and $n_v^o$ indicate object count of $O$ in $p$ and $V_0$.
elements.sent-2411-266: For example, in \cref{fig:main1 , for the question ``Is there one bear?'' $b^{\text{bear _c=1$ when $n^{\text{bear _p=1$ and $n^{\text{bear _v=1$ (when both video $V_0$ and prompt $p$ describes one bear, it is marked as correct).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-267: For attribute-related questions $Q^o_a$, we prompt MLLM to analyze the initial video and provide a binary answer (yes=1 and no=0), denoted as $A_a^o=\{b_a^o\}$, to evaluate the alignment between the objects in $V_0$ and those specified in the prompt $p$.
elements.sent-2411-268: To this end, we first identify the key object $O^*$ and determine each number of its instances to be preserved.
elements.sent-2411-269: First, to select which object (\ie, object name) should be retained, we prompt MLLM with question-answer pairs and the initial video as input, allowing it to identify correctly generated objects to preserve.
elements.sent-2411-270: Note that \ours{ can preserve multiple objects in $O^*$ based on the MLLM's decision, as shown in \cref{fig:main1 (Right Top).}}
elements.sent-2411-271: Next, to determine the number of instance in $O^*$ to retain, we define the count of $O^*$ as $N^*$ based on the previous triplet $A^{o^* _c=\{b^{o^* _c, n^{o^* _p, n^{o^* _v\}$: \begin{equation}N^*=\begin{cases}n_p^{o^*} \text{if}\; n^{o^*}_p\leq n^{o^*}_v n^{o^*}_v \text{otherwise,} \end{cases}\end{equation} where $n_p^{o^* <n_v^{o^*}$ indicates the need to remove excess instances of the object, and $n^{o^* _p>n^{o^* _v$ suggests that additional instances are required.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-274: To localize the correctly generated $O^*$ regions, we propose a Region-Preserving Segmentation (RPS) module, which combines Molmo~\cite{deitke2024molmo , a VLM that can localize specific objects by referring 2d points in an image given a text-pointing prompt, and Semantic-SAM~\cite{li2023semantic , a segmentation model receiving point as its input.}}
elements.sent-2411-275: Given the set of preserved objects $O^*$ and their corresponding quantities $N^*$, we first generate a pointing prompt for Molmo using the template: `Point the biggest $\{N^*\}$ $\{O^*\}$' (\eg, "Point the biggest 1 bear").
elements.sent-2411-276: Considering efficiency, we sample frames from $V_0$ at four-frame intervals (\eg{ , uniformly sampled 4 out of 16 frames) and input each sampled frame along with the pointing prompt into Molmo, obtaining 2D coordinates indicating the location of $O^*$ in each frame.}
elements.sent-2411-277: Leveraging these 2D points, we employ Semantic-SAM to segment the corresponding regions, producing binary segmentation masks.
elements.sent-2411-278: The final segmentation mask is denoted as $\mathbf{m}\in\mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times (T/4)}$.}
elements.sent-2411-279: To obtain the final frame-wise mask $\mathbf{M}\in\mathbb{R ^{H \times W \times T}$, we replicate each mask $m$ along the temporal axis to fill the missing frames.}
elements.sent-2411-280: Given the region to refine $\mathbf{M}$, we introduce a selective noise re-sampling process to enable controlled regeneration of specific regions.
elements.sent-2411-281: To process the pixel-level, multi-frame mask $\mathbf{M}$ into the latent space, we transform $\mathbf{M}$ from pixel space to latent space through block averaging (\ie{ , pooling).}
elements.sent-2411-282: Specifically, we compute the mean of each $|H/d| \times |W/d|$ submatrix within $\mathbf{M}$, where $d$ denotes the downsampling scale factor, effectively reducing the spatial resolution of the mask.
elements.sent-2411-283: The combined noise map $\epsilon_0^*$ is then computed as follows: \begin{equation}\epsilon^*_0 = \left(\epsilon_0 \otimes \text{pool}\left(\mathbf{M}, d\right)\right) + \left(\epsilon'_0 \otimes \left(1 - \text{pool}\left(\mathbf{M}, d\right)\right)\right),\end{equation} where $\text{pool (\cdot, d): \mathbb{R ^{H\times W \times T}\rightarrow\mathbb{R ^{|H/d| \times |W/d| \times T}$ denotes the block averaging operation with scale factor $d$, and $\otimes$ represents element-wise multiplication, which preserves the initial noise map structure in the masked regions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-284: By using this hybrid noise map $\epsilon_0^*$ as input to a frozen video diffusion model with corresponding localized prompts, we enable targeted refinement within designated regions.
elements.sent-2411-285: This approach helps produce high-fidelity video generation with controlled updates that precisely align with the intended modifications.. {.}
